theory.intro: A 3-D object looks different from different directions. We can draw its **top**, **front** and **side** views as flat 2-D shapes.
facts: The **top view** (or plan) is what you see looking straight down.
The **front view** is what you see looking straight at the front.
The **side view** is what you see looking from the left or right.
Each view is a flat outline made of squares; hidden cubes behind others are not shown as extra squares.
steps: Choose the direction (top, front or side).
Imagine flattening the object onto that wall or floor.
Draw the outline you would see as a grid of squares.

foundation.q: How many squares are in the **top view** of a single cube?
A row of $3$ cubes sits side by side. What is the **front view**?
A tower of $2$ cubes stacked on top of each other. What is the **front view**?
A row of $3$ cubes. What is the **top view**?
A single cube. What is its **side view**?
A $2 \times 2$ flat square of cubes (one layer). What is the **top view**?
A tower of $3$ cubes. What is its **side view**?
A single cube. What is its **top view**?
A row of $4$ cubes. What is the **front view**?
A $2 \times 2$ flat layer of cubes. What is the **front view**?

development.q: An L-shape: $2$ cubes in a row, with $1$ cube stacked on the left one. Give the **front view**.
development.a: an L of squares: bottom row $2$, top-left $1$

development.q: A $3$-cube row. The **top view** and **front view** are both rows of $3$. What is the **side view**?
A staircase: bottom row $3$ cubes, $2$ cubes stacked on the leftmost. Give the **side view**.
development.a: an L: bottom $3$, top-left $1$ extra... a $3$-wide base with $2$-tall left step

development.q: A $2 \times 2$ base with $1$ cube on top of one corner. Give the **top view**.

solution.note: Top cube hides inside the base outline

development.q: A $2 \times 2 \times 2$ cube of $8$ small cubes. Give the **front view**.
A $2 \times 2 \times 2$ cube of $8$ small cubes. Give the **top view**.
A row of $3$ cubes with one cube stacked on the middle one. Give the **front view**.
development.a: bottom row $3$, with $1$ square above the middle

development.q: An object's front view is a $2 \times 1$ row and its side view is $1$ square. How many cubes might it have if $1$ cube deep?
A $1 \times 3$ row standing on its end (a $3$-tall tower). Give the **top view**.
An L-shape of $3$ cubes (2 along the bottom, 1 up on the right). Give the **side view** if it is $1$ cube deep.

mastery.q: An object's top view is a $2 \times 2$ square and its front view is a $2 \times 2$ square. What is the smallest number of cubes that fits?

solution.note: One layer covers the top view
Still as few as 4 if a back column is 2 tall

mastery.q: Front view: a $3 \times 1$ row. Side view: a $1 \times 1$ square. Top view: a $3 \times 1$ row. Describe the object.
A staircase has front view: bottom $3$, then $2$, then $1$ (steps). How many cubes if $1$ deep?
An object's three views are all single squares. What is the object?
Top view: $2 \times 1$. Front view: $2 \times 2$. Side view: $1 \times 2$. How many cubes?
Two objects have the same front view ($2 \times 1$) but different top views. Give a top view for each.
mastery.a: e.g. one a $2 \times 1$ row, the other an L-shape

mastery.q: An object is $2$ wide, $1$ deep, with heights $1, 2$ across the front. Sketch the front view.
mastery.a: left square $1$ tall, right column $2$ tall

mastery.q: The top view is a $3 \times 2$ rectangle and the object is $1$ cube high. How many cubes?
Front view $2 \times 2$, side view $2 \times 2$, top view $2 \times 2$ — a solid block. How many cubes?
An object's front and side views are both $1 \times 3$ columns and the top view is $1$ square. Describe it.
